questions.question: What is the key difference between containers and virtual machines?
options: Containers run on Windows only, while VMs run on Linux only
Containers share the host kernel and package only the app and dependencies, while VMs run a full guest operating system with its own kernel
Containers are more secure than VMs because they use encryption
VMs start faster than containers because they are smaller
questions.explanation: Containers share the host machine's kernel and only include the application code, libraries, and configuration. VMs run a complete guest operating system with its own kernel, making them heavier (gigabytes vs megabytes) and slower to start (minutes vs seconds).

questions.question: Why should you copy requirements.txt and run pip install BEFORE copying the rest of your application code in a Dockerfile?
options: Python requires dependencies to be installed before any code files exist
It prevents Docker from accessing your source code during the build
Docker caches each layer, so if your code changes but dependencies don't, the pip install layer is reused from cache instead of re-running
It reduces the final image size by 50%
questions.explanation: Docker caches each instruction as a layer. If a layer's input hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached result. By copying requirements.txt separately, a code change only invalidates the COPY . . layer and beyond, not the slow pip install layer. This dramatically speeds up rebuilds.

questions.question: In a docker-compose.yml file, how does the Django backend service connect to the PostgreSQL database service named 'db'?
options: Using localhost:5432 as the hostname
Using the container's IP address, which must be hardcoded
Using 'db' as the hostname (the service name), since Docker Compose creates a shared network where services are reachable by name
Using 127.0.0.1:5432, which Docker maps automatically
questions.explanation: Docker Compose creates a shared network for all services defined in the file. Each service is reachable by its service name as the hostname. So the Django backend connects to PostgreSQL using 'db' as the hostname (e.g., postgres://user:pass@db:5432/dbname).

questions.question: Which GitHub Actions trigger would run a workflow only when a pull request is opened or updated against the main branch?
options: on: push
on: pull_request: branches: [main]
on: merge: branches: [main]
on: commit: branches: [main]
questions.explanation: The pull_request trigger with a branches filter runs the workflow when a PR targeting the specified branch is opened, synchronized (new commits pushed), or reopened. There is no 'merge' or 'commit' trigger in GitHub Actions.

questions.question: What is the difference between Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Deployment (CD)?
options: CI runs tests locally, while CD runs tests on a server
CI automatically builds and tests code on every push to catch integration problems early. CD automatically deploys code to production after it passes all checks.
CI and CD are the same thing with different names
CI deploys to staging, while CD deploys to production
questions.explanation: CI focuses on validating code quality by automatically running builds, tests, linters, and type checks on every push or pull request. CD extends this by automatically deploying the validated code to production (or a staging environment). CI answers 'is this safe to merge?' while CD answers 'how do we ship it?'

questions.question: How does dependency caching in GitHub Actions speed up CI workflows?
options: It skips the testing step if dependencies haven't changed
It stores installed packages between workflow runs and restores them when the lock file hasn't changed, avoiding repeated downloads and installations
It compresses the source code to reduce transfer time
It runs multiple workflows in parallel on the same runner
questions.explanation: Dependency caching stores the npm or pip cache directory between runs. When the lock file (package-lock.json or requirements.txt) hasn't changed, the cached packages are restored instead of downloaded and installed from scratch. This can save minutes on each CI run.

questions.question: Why is Vercel recommended for deploying the Next.js frontend while Railway is recommended for the Django backend?
options: Vercel is free and Railway is paid
Vercel was built by the Next.js team and has seamless integration (preview deploys, edge functions, automatic optimization), while Railway provides better Python support and built-in PostgreSQL
Railway doesn't support JavaScript applications
Vercel includes a built-in database that Django requires
questions.explanation: Vercel was created by the same team that builds Next.js, so it offers the best integration with features like automatic preview deployments, edge rendering, and zero-config builds. Railway excels at running backend services with built-in database provisioning and CLI-based deployment, making it well suited for Django apps.

questions.question: What does Sentry provide that standard logging does not?
options: Sentry writes logs to a file, while standard logging only prints to the console
Sentry captures unhandled exceptions with full stack traces, groups similar errors, tracks error frequency over time, and provides browser and request context for each error
Sentry replaces the need for any logging in your application
Sentry only works in development, not in production
questions.explanation: Sentry goes beyond logging by capturing the full context of errors: stack traces with local variables, browser information, user actions leading up to the crash (breadcrumbs), and request details. It also groups similar errors, tracks whether they are new or recurring, and provides performance monitoring.

questions.question: What should a production health check endpoint verify, and what HTTP status code should it return when a critical dependency fails?
options: It should verify the server is running and return 200 regardless of dependency status
It should verify the server and critical dependencies (like the database) are operational, returning 200 when healthy and 503 (Service Unavailable) when a dependency fails
It should return 500 when any error occurs and 201 when healthy
It should only check memory usage and return 429 if memory is low
questions.explanation: A production health check should verify both the application server and critical dependencies like the database. Returning 503 (Service Unavailable) when a dependency is down tells load balancers and monitoring tools that the service is unhealthy, allowing them to route traffic elsewhere or trigger alerts.

questions.question: Why should sensitive values like API keys and database passwords be stored as GitHub Actions secrets rather than in workflow YAML files?
options: YAML files cannot contain special characters that are common in passwords
Secrets are encrypted at rest, masked in logs, and not accessible to workflows triggered by forks, preventing credential exposure in version control and CI output
GitHub charges extra for workflows that contain hardcoded values
Secrets make workflows run faster because they are pre-loaded
questions.explanation: GitHub Actions secrets are encrypted at rest and injected at runtime. They are automatically masked in workflow logs (replaced with ***) and are not available to pull request workflows triggered from forked repositories. Hardcoding secrets in YAML files exposes them in version control history, making them visible to anyone with repo access.
